skill.name: commit-helper
skill.description: Conventional commit assistant for Logseq Template Graph. It reviews staged git changes, selects an appropriate type and scope, and drafts commit messages that follow project rules.
skill.summary: Drafts and validates Conventional Commit messages for Logseq Template Graph changes.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, weak crypto, PowerShell, and network patterns. Review found the command patterns are legitimate git workflow guidance, while the weak crypto and PowerShell hits are Markdown and template false positives. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Git Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to use Bash for git status and diff inspection, then suggests git commit commands. This is expected for a commit helper, but it can read repository diffs and create commits when commands are executed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines explicitly direct Bash use for git inspection and show git commit commands. The context is a commit assistant, so the risk is real but consistent with the declared purpose.

medium_findings.title: Copy-Paste Shell Commit Templates
medium_findings.description: Several examples use shell command substitution and heredoc syntax to create multiline commits. The quoted heredoc pattern reduces shell expansion risk, but users should review generated commands before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact shell patterns are present in Markdown examples and can execute git commit if copied. They are not hidden or malicious, but they are still executable shell guidance.

low_findings.title: External Documentation Link Is Benign
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points to the public Conventional Commits documentation. It is a Markdown reference link, not code that performs a network request.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains only a Markdown documentation link. No fetch, curl, wget, or other network call was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-crypto hits occur in prose, placeholders, and Conventional Commit examples such as description rules. No cryptographic APIs, hash implementations, or encryption routines were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations define commit-message description style and validation rules, not cryptographic behavior. The repository contains Markdown and text templates rather than executable crypto code.

low_findings.title: PowerShell Detection Is Documentation Context
low_findings.description: The PowerShell hit appears inside an example diff and a proposed documentation commit body. It does not instruct the skill to run PowerShell during normal operation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are inside a Markdown example showing documentation changes. They are not part of an operational workflow or executable script.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bash-Based Git Inspection
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill asks the assistant to run git status and git diff commands to inspect repository changes. This accesses repository content and should be limited to trusted workspaces.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly listed in Bash code blocks. They are read-only git commands, but they expose local diffs to the assistant.

dangerous_patterns.title: Generated Git Commit Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill outputs git commit commands for users to copy and run. This can alter repository history if executed without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines show concrete git commit command examples. The behavior is explicit and relevant to the skill purpose.

content.value_statement: Writing clear commit messages takes time and often leads to inconsistent history. This skill reviews git changes and drafts Conventional Commit messages with project-specific types and scopes.
actual_capabilities: Reviews staged and unstaged git changes using status and diff commands.
Chooses Conventional Commit types such as feat, fix, docs, build, and chore.
Applies project-specific scopes including classes, properties, templates, ci, and scripts.
Drafts short commit subjects and optional bodies for larger changes.
Flags breaking changes and formats migration notes when needed.
Validates common commit-message mistakes such as vague wording and trailing periods.
limitations: Designed for the Logseq Template Graph project conventions.
Requires access to git status and diff output for best results.
Generated commit commands should be reviewed before execution.
Does not replace human review for release notes or migration impact.

use_cases.title: Prepare Schema Template Commits
use_cases.description: Draft focused commit messages for class, property, and template changes in Logseq Template Graph modules.

use_cases.title: Validate Commit Style
use_cases.description: Check whether a proposed message follows Conventional Commit format, scope rules, and concise wording.

use_cases.title: Document Breaking Changes
use_cases.description: Create commit bodies and footers that explain removed classes, changed properties, and migration steps.

prompt_templates.title: Suggest a Commit Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my staged changes and suggest one Conventional Commit message for this project.

prompt_templates.title: Validate My Message
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate this commit message against the project rules and suggest a corrected version if needed: [paste message].

prompt_templates.title: Compare Commit Options
prompt_templates.scenario: Choose between multiple types or scopes.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my staged changes and give three commit message options with different scopes or levels of detail.

prompt_templates.title: Handle a Breaking Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the staged changes for breaking changes and draft a Conventional Commit with body, footer, and migration notes.

output_examples.input: Staged changes add a Recipe class, cooking properties, and a module README update.
output_examples.output: A feature commit using the classes scope, plus a short body summarizing the new class, properties, parent class, and documentation update.

output_examples.input: A draft message says: Added files.
output_examples.output: A corrected Conventional Commit subject with an imperative verb, a specific scope, and no trailing period.

output_examples.input: Staged changes remove a deprecated Customer class and add replacement guidance.
output_examples.output: A breaking-change commit with a clear subject, a BREAKING CHANGE footer, and migration steps for existing graphs.

best_practices: Stage related changes together before asking for a final commit message.
Review the suggested type, scope, and command before running git commit.
Use a body when the reason for the change is not obvious from the diff.
anti_patterns: Do not use vague subjects such as update files or fix bug.
Do not combine unrelated schema, documentation, and build changes in one commit.
Do not execute generated shell commands without checking the final message.

faq.question: Does this skill make commits automatically?
faq.answer: It suggests commit messages and commands. A user or agent still decides whether to run the command.

faq.question: What commit format does it use?
faq.answer: It uses Conventional Commits with project-specific type and scope guidance.

faq.question: Can it work outside Logseq Template Graph?
faq.answer: It can provide general Conventional Commit help, but its scope rules are tailored to this project.

faq.question: Why does it need git diff output?
faq.answer: The diff shows what changed, which helps choose the correct type, scope, and level of detail.

faq.question: How does it handle breaking changes?
faq.answer: It marks breaking subjects with an exclamation mark and adds a BREAKING CHANGE footer when appropriate.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning that it uses git and shell command guidance. No malicious behavior was found.
